key_insight: The paper introduces interval and fuzzy physics-augmented neural networks that learn physics-consistent lower, mean, and upper hyperelastic energy responses to quantify and propagate uncertainty in constitutive modeling.
engineering_relevance: This gives mechanics simulations a compact, distribution-free way to bound noisy stress-strain data and carry those uncertainty bounds into finite element analyses.
method: iPANN learns sparse lower/mean/upper free-energy branches; stresses are obtained by automatic differentiation.
fPANN converts the interval model into nested fuzzy responses using alpha-cut interpolation.
A two-stage transfer-learning strategy first fits a sparse mean model, then fine-tunes bound branches with smoothed L0 regularization and physics constraints.
evidence_signal: The approach is evaluated on synthetic isotropic hyperelastic data with heteroscedastic noise, varying random realizations, shifted noise means, and varying noise magnitudes, and the learned bounds enclose noisy observations while generalizing to test data.
watch_item: The framework is demonstrated on synthetic hyperelastic problems, so performance and calibration on real experimental constitutive datasets still need verification.
paper_title: Interval and fuzzy physics-augmented neural networks (iPANN and fPANN) for uncertainty quantification and propagation in constitutive modeling
